categories.summary: Today’s neurology papers point to three practical advances: a way to personalize ICU care after ischemic stroke, a promising new rehab-style approach for multiple sclerosis, and clear gaps in contraceptive care for people with neurological and developmental disabilities. In the stroke study, researchers grouped early vital-sign patterns into three phenotypes (one with fast heart and breathing rates that carried much higher mortality) that outperformed traditional severity scores and even suggested different fluid and sedative dosing might be beneficial for different groups. A small 12-week intermittent hypoxia (simulated high-altitude) program in mostly progressive MS was safe, well tolerated, and showed hints of clinical benefit, supporting larger trials. And a nationwide Medicaid review found people with epilepsy were slightly more likely to get contraception while those with intellectual and developmental disabilities were much less likely, underscoring the need for better, individualized reproductive care.
